Background
T﻿he definition of cerebral palsy (CP) has constantly evolved since 
the 1800s and has involved some of the most influential medical 
minds in history, including William Little, Sir William Osler, and 
Sigmund Freud. Advances in medicine and worldwide communi-
cation have led to the current definition: “Cerebral palsy describes 
a group of permanent disorders of the development of movement 
and posture, causing activity limitation, which are attributed to 
nonprogressive disturbances that occurred in the developing fetal 
or infant brain. T﻿he motor disorders of CP are often accompanied 
by disturbances of sensation, perception, cognition, communica -
tion, and behavior, by epilepsy, and by secondary musculoskeletal 
problems.”103 T﻿his definition excludes a number of disease pro -
cesses that may present similarly, but either progress (i.e., genetic 
or metabolic diseases), have a primarily peripheral etiology (i.e., 
neuromuscular or peripheral nerve injury), or occur after the 
central nervous system has fully developed (i.e., traumatic injuries 
or oncologic manifestations). It serves to remind us that the term 
CP reflects much more than a musculoskeletal process; it reaches 
to the function of a person. For example, each of the following 
cases is a possible presentation of a child with CP:
AB is a 10-year-old, left-handed female who was born at 36 weeks 
of gestation and whose brain imaging showed a prenatal stroke 
on the left. She has difficulty using her right arm and leg  
because of right spastic hemiplegia for which she receives botu-
linum toxin injections three times a year. She uses an ankle-
foot orthosis to run and play soccer with her friends. She is in 
regular classes at school and has no problem seeing, hearing, 
eating, talking, or socializing. She does note that she gets a 
“little upset” because she cannot keep up with her friends on 
the playground.
CD is a 10-year-old male who was born at 32 weeks of gestation 
and whose brain imaging showed significant periventricular 
leukomalacia. He has difficulty using both of his legs to walk 
because of spastic diplegia, for which he receives botulinum 
toxin injections three times a year and takes an oral medica -
tion three times a day. He requires bilateral ankle-foot ortho -
ses and a reverse walker for ambulation in the community; at 
home, he crawls to get around. In addition, he has difficulty 
coordinating fine motor tasks with either hand but can put 
on his clothes with assistance for buttons and zippers. He 
requires accommodations at school for his mobility and dys -
arthria, needs glasses, and has difficulty being understood 
when talking. He has no problems with hearing or eating.  
He expresses that he wants other “little boys at school to play 
with him.”
EF is a 10-year-old male who was born at 24 weeks of gestation 
and whose brain imaging showed diffuse encephalomalacia 
• Tone management is a hallmark aspect of CP management  
and can include the use of therapy and bracing, enteral 
medications, injections, and surgical interventions.
• Hip subluxation can be a common progressive disorder in  
CP , occurring at higher rates in patients with higher Gross 
Motor Function Classification System scores.
• Neuromuscular scoliosis is a common progressive disorder in 
CP , which occurs at higher rates in individuals who are more 
severely affected.
• Physiatrists should work with individuals with CP and their 
families during adolescence as they transition to adulthood and 
be aware of their higher risk of stroke, myelopathy, loss of 
mobility, dementia, pain, fatigue, sleep disorders, and mood 
disorders.
48
Cerebral Palsy
DESIREE L. ROGE, JARED LEVIN, AND ELAINE Y. TSAO
• Cerebral palsy (CP) is a spectrum disorder most commonly 
caused by prematurity related to ischemic and hemorrhagic 
injury along the periventricular tracts. It is defined by a 
nonprogressive disturbance of the brain that results in at least 
abnormalities of movement and posture.
• CP is commonly classified by body part(s) affected, type of 
movement or tone anomaly, and degree of function.
• The Prechtl General Movement Assessment and Hammersmith 
Infant Neurological Examination are tools used to predict and 
diagnose CP in neonates before 6 months of age; they are used 
best in combination with brain imaging.
• Positive prognostic indicators for function after CP are sitting 
independently by age 2 years, having less than three primitive 
reflexes by 24 months, having high visual and cognitive 
function, and lack of epilepsy.
SUMMARY PEARLS

and hydrocephalus that required shunting. He has difficulty 
using all of his limbs because of spastic quadriplegia, for 
which he receives botulinum injections four times a year and 
takes two oral medications three times a day (one for spastic -
ity and one for dystonia). He is mobile only in a customized 
wheelchair and requires bilateral ankle-foot orthoses and  
bilateral resting hand splints for positioning. He has numer-
ous other impairments, including scoliosis, dysphagia, nutri -
tional compromise requiring a gastrostomy tube for feeding 
and hydration, constipation, and significant intellectual  
disability.
Epidemiology
CP is the most common cause of disability in developed coun -
tries. T﻿he direct and indirect costs associated with CP are three 
to four times that of a neurotypical child. 51 T﻿he global birth 
prevalence of CP ranges from 1.6 to 3.4 per 1000 live births. 
Low- and middle-income countries have reported birth preva-
lence in the higher range, whereas high-income countries have 
observed a declining trend in prevalence since the mid-2000s. 
T﻿his difference in international CP birth prevalence likely reflects 
the widely varied access to pre- and perinatal care. 63 When the 
overall prevalence is examined more closely, dramatic increases 
are seen in children with very low birth weight (59.6 per 1000 
live births) and very early gestation (111.8 per 1000 live 
births).
86 Research has found the germinal matrix surrounding 
the ventricles is at increased risk of ischemic or hemorrhagic  
injury caused by the immature vasculature and fluctuations in 
cerebral blood flow.
4
Etiology/Risk Factors
Many times, the etiology of the brain injury associated with  
the clinical findings of CP is clear, as in a history of placental 
abruption with significant cardiorespiratory compromise causing 
global hypoxic ischemic injury resulting in spastic quadriplegia. 
However, it may not be as clear in a near-term infant with an 
uncomplicated hospital course who is developmentally delayed. 
Data from CP registries reveal that a larger portion of children 
with CP have a multifactorial etiology from a mixture of known 
risk factors.
75 T﻿here are five major categories of risk factors associ-
ated with the diagnosis of CP ( Table 48.1).
T﻿here is increasing evidence for genomic variants contributing 
to CP etiology. Advances in sequencing technologies have allowed 
rapid and cost-effective genome or exome sequencing, revealing 
causative gene mutations in CP syndromes (GAD1, KANK1, or 
adaptor protein complex-4), novel gene mutation (ZC4H2), or 
pathologic single-nucleotide variants.
43,72 Studies using chromo -
somal microarray analysis uncovered rare gene-level copy number 
variants associated with CP .
87,114,134,137 A systematic review and 
meta-analysis by Gonzalez-Mantilla et al. demonstrated a 31.1% 
genetic diagnostic yield in CP in pediatric and adult patients, 
similar to rates of other neurodevelopmental disorders for which 
exome sequencing is standard of care. T﻿he findings provided evi-
dence in support of the inclusion of CP in exome sequencing as 
part of the standard of care in diagnostic evaluation of patients 
with neurodevelopmental disorders.
35
Classification
One classification system for children with CP uses the topography 
or distribution of affected limbs. In this system, hemiplegic CP  
(Fig. 48.1) refers to a unilateral upper and lower limb distribution. 
It is common in term and near-term infants and associated with 
focal brain pathology (unilateral strokes, asymmetric periventricular 
leukomalacia [PVL], or congenital malformations). Quadriplegic 
CP or tetraplegic CP (Fig. 48.2) refers to a bilateral upper and lower 
limb distribution and is associated with diffuse brain pathology 
(cortical, subcortical, and intraventricular lesions) ( Table 48.2).

signs are defined as involuntary increases in frequency or magni-
tude of muscle activity. T﻿hey include hypertonia (“examiner  
induced abnormally increased resistance to movement about a 
joint”)109 and hyperkinesia (“unwanted, excess movement by the 
child”).107 Hypertonia is further subdivided into spastic, dystonic, 
and rigid hypertonia. Hyperkinetic movements are divided into 
dystonia, chorea, athetosis, myoclonus, tremor, tics, and stereoty-
pies. Negative motor signs are described as “lack of muscle activity 
or control” and encompass weakness, reduced selective motor 
control, ataxia, and apraxia (Box 48.1).
108
T﻿he push for standardized terminology has benefitted from the 
development of many CP registries across the globe. One impor -
tant network, the Surveillance of Cerebral Palsy in Europe 
(SCPE), has standardized terminology and a reporting structure. 
T﻿heir terms include unilateral spastic CP , bilateral spastic CP , dys-
tonic CP , choreoathetoid CP , ataxic CP , and nonclassifiable.14
It is important to note that, although one topographic distribu-
tion or motor sign may predominate, CP is commonly more com-
plicated, and multiple motor signs and distributions may be seen in 
each patient. Another important aspect of describing the clinical 
findings associated with CP is functional. T﻿he Gross Motor Func-
tion Classification System (GMFCS) is a well-accepted and widely 
used classification system. It was revised in 2006 and has been 
shown to be valid and reliable. T﻿his system quantifies the typical

(Table 48.3).
In recognition that clinical findings in CP also include distur -
bances of sensation, perception, cognition, communication, and 
behavior, a multifaceted classification system was proposed in 
2006. It recommended defining CP in terms of four components: 
motor abnormalities, accompanying impairments, anatomic and 
neuroimaging findings, and causation and timing.
103
In 2007, the World Health Organization (WHO), in collabo-
ration with many other organizations, published the International 
Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health for Children 
and Youth
 (ICF-CY). T﻿his was an expansion of the original ICF 
for children from birth to 18 years of age to specifically address 
the developing child, the developmental delay, and the child in 
the context of the family. T﻿he ICF-CY provides internationally 
accepted terminology and classifications that focus on the rela-
tionship between structure and function and moves from a medi-
cal model of disability to a biopsychosocial model of care  
(Fig. 48.5).
112,133 In this model, biopsychosocial function is bro-
ken down into five categories: body function, body structure,  
activity and participation, environmental factors, and personal 
factors.
Diagnosis
T﻿he term CP does not reflect a specific etiology; it is a description 
of clinical findings. T﻿here are many disease processes that can 
present as CP . T﻿he initial evaluation of a child with suspected  
CP should determine the diagnosis (if possible), decide which 
tests are needed to make the diagnosis, determine the etiology of  
the symptoms, determine comorbidities, and develop treatment  
options. Early diagnosis or identification of infants at high risk of 
CP allows early intervention that may potentially promote cogni-
tive and motor development. Several assessment tools have been 
utilized to predict CP risk in infants.
T﻿he Prechtl General Movement Assessment (GMA) is based on 
observation of infants’ spontaneous movements, known as general 
movements (GMs), that persist until 4 to 5 months of corrected 
age. T﻿he GMs of an infant comprise of a “writhing” phase from 
early fetal life up to 6 to 9 weeks of corrected age, then “fidgety” 
phase occurs 6 to 9 weeks of corrected age through 20 weeks of 
corrected age. Abnormal writhing movements are characterized  
as poor repertoire, cramped synchronized, or chaotic, whereas 
fidgety movements are classified as normal, abnormal, sporadic, or 
absent.
26 Although false positives are high with writhing GMs with 
a high sensitivity of 93% to 100% but low specificity of 40% to 
59%, studies have demonstrated sensitivity of 95% to 100% and 
specificity of 89% to 98% for fidgety GMs for predicting CP at  
2 years of age.
56,117 T﻿he GMA is a more predictive tool during the 
fidgety period, with the most accurate time for assessment at 12 to 
14 weeks of corrected age.
84
T﻿he Hammersmith Infant Neurological Examination (HINE) 
is a validated tool using scorable standardized neurologic exami -
nation to identify infants between 2 and 24 months of age who 
are at risk for CP . T﻿he examination includes 26 items, evaluating 
infants’ cranial nerves, posture, movement, tone, and reflexes. 102 
A widely accepted cutoff HINE score of less than 57 of a maxi -
mum 78 at 3 months of corrected age is predictive of CP with  
a high sensitivity of 90% to 96% and specificity of 85% to 
87%.
40,82,102 T﻿he Hammersmith Neonatal Neurological Examina-
tion (HNNE) is another screening tool to detect neurologic  
differences in preterm and term-born infants up to 3 months

A combination of GMA, HINE, and brain magnetic reso -
nance imaging (MRI) is currently the recommended toolset  
for predicting CP with the highest predictive validity before  
5 months of corrected age.10,40,82 After 5 months of corrected age, 
MRI, HINE, and the Developmental Assessment of Young Chil-
dren (a parent report measure of adaptive behavior, cognitive, 
social-emotional, physical, and communication domains) are the 
most predictive tools for CP detection.
82 T o assist with early  
detection of CP or identification of high risk, Novak et al. pub -
lished an algorithm of best available evidence pathways, consist -
ing of clinical history and presentation, standardized assessment 
tools, and neuroimaging.
82 Before commencing any examination 
of an individual with suspected CP , it is important to gauge the 
emotional environment and the family’s understanding of the 
child’s current medical issues. Although many families bring 
their child to be evaluated because they believe something is dif -
ferent or wrong, they can be overwhelmed and afraid that their 
fears will be confirmed.
History
A detailed history should include all basic attributes of a medical 
history, the individual’s birth history, family history of blood clots 
or stroke, and any possible risk factors for CP (see earlier). T﻿he 
developmental history should include when and how a milestone 
was achieved. For example, a child may have crawled at 9 months, 
but there are different patterns of crawling (e.g., “commando 
crawling,” in which the child pulls the body across the floor with

hip and knees but no reciprocal motions below the waist). Each 
of these crawling patterns can be associated with increased muscle

ment, this suggests a neurodegenerative process and may indicate 
that a metabolic or genetic disease workup is also needed. A func-

T﻿his should include discussing the individual’s mobility in the 
home and community setting and participation in activities of 
daily living and community activities.
Clinical Findings and Patterns
A thorough medical examination should include detailed neuro -
logic, musculoskeletal, and functional components. Areas of 
weakness as well as tonal and movement abnormalities should be 
identified. A child may have multiple tonal abnormalities such as 
spasticity, dystonia, and truncal hypotonia. In addition to positive 
and negative motor signs, the persistence of primitive reflexes is a 
common finding in individuals with CP ( Table 48.4).
135 Having 
individuals with suspected CP demonstrate the extent of their 
mobility, object handling, transfers, and communication will 
provide valuable information.
Neonatal Pattern
After a severe brain injury, neonates tend to be hypotonic and 
develop hypertonia over time. It can be difficult to elicit muscle 
stretch reflexes, and there are minimal milestones to evaluate. 
Milestones may be delayed in premature children until the age of 
2 years. Repeat examinations should be completed regularly.
Diplegic Pattern
Hypertonic deficits are noted mainly in bilateral lower limbs, 
though upper limbs may be involved. Scissoring of bilateral lower 
limbs and talipes equinovarus deformities predominate. If ambula-
tory, then toe walking, toe walking with knees flexed, or crouching 
tends to be present. Loss of range of motion (ROM) and hyper -
reflexia is present in the lower limbs. In a recent review, 72% of 
children with this pattern had neuroimaging that revealed changes 
on MRI.
99 Among children with CP , 38% will have this pattern.81
Hemiplegic Pattern
Hypertonic focal deficits, early hand lateralization, loss of ROM 
in affected limbs, and asymmetric limb use are associated with

recent review, 84% of children with this pattern had neuroimag -

Quadriplegic Pattern
T﻿here is diffuse spastic hypertonicity with truncal dystonia, persis-
tent primitive reflexes, intellectual disability, hyperreflexia, and 
loss of ROM in all affected limbs. In a recent review, 92% of 
children with this pattern had neuroimaging that revealed MRI 
changes. Among children with CP , 23% will have this pattern.81 
T﻿he clinical presentation will guide the needed workup and devel-
opment of a comprehensive treatment plan. For example, a pre -
natal stroke could cause a hemiplegic pattern indicating the need 
for a coagulation workup and neuroimaging to evaluate for a 
brain malformation, as is found in 10% of children with this pat-
tern.99 Although diffuse hypotonia may be a precursor to hyper -
tonia, persistent hypotonia is often associated with genetic or 
metabolic syndromes.
Functional Prognosis
In the 1970s, Molnar et al. laid the foundation for the future  
development of motor curves based on developmental milestone 
achievement. T﻿heir work showed that sitting independently by the 
age of 2 years or having less than three primitive reflexes present by 
18 to 24 months was a positive predictor of ambulation, although 
not sitting independently by 4 years was a negative predictor. 69  
A systematic review and meta-analysis of observational studies 
demonstrated that other positive predictors of ambulation include 
absence of visual impairment, absence of intellectual disability, and 
absence of epilepsy or seizures, in addition to sitting independently 
by age of 2 years.52 Later work by Molnar et al. demonstrated that 
over 50% of all children with CP will ambulate, and they further 
divided this by topographic classification (80%–90% diplegia, 
50% quadriplegia, 75% dyskinesia).70 In 2008, these findings were 
replicated in a large European population of children with CP .6
Moving from the prediction of ambulation to the prediction of 
longitudinal gross motor function in children with CP , Rosen -
baum et al. published motor development curves based on an 
individual’s GMFCS level in 2002.104 T﻿hese curves helped transi-
tion the diagnosis of CP from a disease of childhood to a chronic 
disease. T﻿hey have been validated, and their stability has been 
shown since their initial publication. Interestingly, children with 
GMFCS levels I and V often remained at these levels, while chil -
dren with GMFCS levels II, III, and IV needed reclassification 
over time.104 T﻿hese findings were replicated in a large cohort in 
Europe.83 Reclassification of MACS and CFCS is also more likely 
to occur in younger children (age #4 years), particularly for those 
whose initial GMFCS level was II through IV , initial MACS level 
was III, or initial CFCS was II through V .91 As the ICF-CY model 
continues to modify our paradigm of health, efforts to assess chil-
dren with CP in the domain of activity and participation will 
likely increase. Most recently, the SCPE has been working to  
develop development curves for self-care capabilities,54 social par-
ticipation,123 and health-related quality of life.123
Medical Management
T﻿he spectrum of associated medical conditions affecting individu-
als with CP is broad. In the last consensus definition of CP , there 
is a greater emphasis on recognizing associated impairments and 
functional limitations as well as coexisting medical conditions, 
aside from the primary abnormalities in movement and posture.103 
Function, participation, and quality of life are often influenced by 
these comorbidities. It is intuitive that the more severe the motor 
disorder or higher GMFCS level, the greater the likelihood that 
the child will have multiple or more severe medical comorbidities. 
T wo exceptions to this correlate are pain occurring in all levels of 
disability and across the lifespan and behavior disorders seen more 
commonly in cases of milder motor disability. 79 Etiologies of CP 
and their anatomic correlates may also predict these associated 
conditions. Some associated conditions, such as seizures, are clearly 
attributed to a disturbance at the brain level, but others may be 
caused by a combination of abnormalities in neural control  
and sensorimotor deficits. T﻿hough described as a nonprogressive 
condition, aging with CP is associated with declining health and 
function status across neurologic and nonneurologic systems. 130 
Whitney et al. described that among 16,728 adults with CP ,  
the most common comorbidity combinations were cardiorespira-
tory diseases, dysphagia, and fluid/electrolyte disorders; metabolic  
disorders (e.g., diabetes, renal disease, hypertension); neurologic-
related disorders (e.g., dementia, cerebrovascular disease); gastroin-
testinal issues; and orthopedic-related disorders.130
Feeding, Growth, Nutrition, and Gastrointestinal
Dysphagia is common in patients with CP . Impaired oropharyn-
geal strength and coordination place a child at risk for not meet -
ing caloric and fluid requirements because of feeding inefficiency. 
Serious medical complications, such as malnutrition and an in -
creased risk of aspiration events, may occur. Caregivers may share 
that this aspect of the child’s care is burdensome, with stress in -
creasing when the child needs considerable time to eat, and ac -
tivities revolve around feeding schedules. Frequently, children will 
struggle to gain weight only to lose it quickly during an illness. It 
is important to remember that some individuals have silent aspi -
ration and may not generate a protective cough; therefore workup 
for aspiration events should include a formal swallow study. Early 
on, children should receive nutritional counseling and supple -
mental caloric and fluid intake if necessary. Parents may resist 
placement of gastrostomy tubes, especially if the child swallows 
safely, even though feedings may require increased time and ag -
gressive positioning strategies. Oral hygiene may be compro -
mised, and children may have excessive sialorrhea, which can 
contribute to aspiration events and cause secondary skin irrita -
tion. Growth and nutrition may be compromised because of 
limited intake of key nutrients, impaired absorption, and endo -
crine abnormalities. When malnourished, children are at risk for 
growth disturbances, increased infection rates, skin breakdown, 
osteopenia, and ultimately decreased life expectancy.59
Usual tools for weight and nutrition management are often 
not reliable in children with CP . Published guidelines for nutri -
tional surveillance in children with neurodisability recommend 
use of triceps skinfold (TSF), mid-upper arm circumference 
(MUAC), and the derived measure of arm muscle area (AMA), all 
in addition to weight for age. 119 Red flags for undernutrition in 
children with neurologic impairment include physical signs of 
undernutrition; weight for age Z-score below 2; TSF , MUAC, or 
AMA below the 10th percentile; and faltering weight over 
time.119 T﻿he North American Growth in Cerebral Palsy Project 
developed CP-specific growth curves. 48,101 T﻿hese growth charts 
highlight increased mortality rates for children GMFCS levels III

geal reflux disease (GERD) and constipation are common in CP , 
may produce discomfort, and exacerbate hypertonicity. In more 
severe cases, children may have dysmotility with delayed gastric 
emptying and esophagitis from medically refractory GERD.
Pulmonary
Lung disease is an important cause of morbidity and mortality  
in CP . T﻿he leading cause of death in children with CP is respira-
tory illness. For adults with CP , death from respiratory illness is  
14 times higher than for their peers with no disability.11 Cumula-
tive injury to lung architecture may occur because of repeated 
aspiration events, infections, decreased mucociliary clearance, 
kyphoscoliosis, and airway obstruction. Nine risk factors that 
place a child with CP at high risk for having at least one hospital 
admission for chest infections in the next 5 years include GMFCS 
level V (difficulty controlling head and body posture in most 
positions), at least one hospital admission for respiratory illness in 
the past year, oropharyngeal dysphagia, seizures, frequent respira-
tory symptoms, GERD, at least two courses of antibiotics for 
chest infections in the past year, mealtime respiratory symptoms, 
and snoring.34 Evaluation recommendations outlined in a consen-
sus statement focusing on prevention and management of respira-
tory illnesses in individuals with CP underscored need for detailed 
evaluation, including medical and mealtime history, physical 
exam/upper airway evaluation, clinical and video fluoroscopy 
swallow assessments, and (when indicated) computed tomogra -
phy of the chest. It stresses the need to manage risk factors of  
aspiration, including GERD and drooling. Some of the strategies 
discussed include modifying feeding consistencies, positioning, 
equipment to optimize feeding, treatment of GERD, feeding 
tubes, and control of sialorrhea with anticholinergic medications, 
botulinum injections, and sometimes surgery. T﻿he need for  
optimizing airway clearance and chest wall mobility was under -
scored.34 T﻿his can be achieved with suction or chest physiotherapy 
techniques. Devices such as cough-assist machine and vibration 
percussion vests can be used. Children with infections should be 
treated with tailored antibiotics based on sputum cultures. Some 
will require prophylactic antibiotic coverage. Preventative mea -
sures include immunizations because this population is at higher 
risk for infections and complications. Children with CP fre -
quently have sleep-disordered breathing. A history of snoring or 
observed irregular breathing patterns with or without excessive 
daytime somnolence should prompt the practitioner to investi -
gate sleep apnea, which if untreated can contribute to further 
morbidity. Apnea may be central, peripheral, or a combination of 
both. Many children have impaired sleep regulation and may not 
achieve restful sleep because of microaspiration and cough, altered 
light perception, medication side effects impacting natural sleep-
wake cycles, and challenges in achieving comfortable position -
ing.31 Upper airway obstruction while awake and during sleep, 
with resulting hypoventilation, is an important contributor to 
respiratory morbidity and decreased quality of life in individuals 
with CP .9 Management can include surgery, noninvasive ventila -
tion, or tracheostomy in very severe cases. 9 Impaired lung func -
tion and restrictive lung disease are common in children with 
neuromuscular weakness and kyphoscoliosis. Early-onset kypho -
scoliosis interferes with lung development by decreasing the num-
ber of alveoli. Scoliosis surgery may improve restrictive lung  
disease but must be considered carefully given risks associated 
with it and whether it will improve functioning and quality of life.
Neurologic Issues
T﻿he CP population has an increased risk for seizures. T﻿he preva-
lence of epilepsy in CP is estimated to be around 33% to 40%.16,121 
Etiologies for seizures in CP may be related to vascular injury, 
brain malformations, or genetic and epileptic syndromes, among 
others. Seizures occur most frequently in children with a quadripa-
retic clinical presentation, GMFCS levels IV and V , term preg -
nancy, history of hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy, and cognitive 
impairment.121 Children with CP and epilepsy have an increased 
number of hospitalizations and emergency department presenta -
tions compared to children with CP or epilepsy alone.121 Medica-
tion use for epilepsy management may be associated with cognitive 
dulling or other side effects. Some children have hyperkinetic 
movement disorders that mimic seizure activity and may require 
video electroencephalography monitoring to delineate between the 
two conditions. Weaning of seizure medications depends on the 
severity of the seizures and a period, usually 2 years, during which 
the child has been seizure free. Children with CP also have a higher 
incidence of intellectual disability; cognitive impairment is esti -
mated to affect 50%. 22 Nonverbal status and severe intellectual 
disability are seen more commonly in children with a quadriparetic 
clinical presentation. However, it is important to note that some 
children, especially with basal ganglia injury and dyskinesias, may 
have preservation of intellectual abilities despite severe dysarthria. 
It is also important to differentiate the cause of communication 
difficulties (cognitive dysfunction vs. hearing or vision limitations 
vs. dysarthria).1 Children who are higher functioning and mobile 
may still have cognitive dysfunction and learning disabilities  
despite relative preservation of verbal language. Attention-deficit 
disorder and attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder have a preva-
lence of 30% in CP .90 Screening for impaired attention and execu-
tive functioning deficits is recommended. Visual perceptual  
deficits may also affect learning, despite a normal IQ.
Mood disorders, such as anxiety and depression, have a high 
prevalence in children with CP and can impact functioning and 
participation. Mood disorders are amplified in adulthood with an 
incidence of 28% vs. 12% compared to the general population. 
T﻿here is a considerable comorbidity with pain and fatigue in the 
CP adult population.131 Up to 30% of children and adolescents 
with CP have an autism diagnosis and other genetic or neurode -
velopmental disorders.62,127 Individuals with both diagnoses often 
present with other comorbidities, such as intellectual disability, 
behavior, and sleep disorders. Depending on the location in the 
brain, some children will be at higher risk for vision and hearing 
deficits. Prematurity is associated with retinal damage and myopia. 
Overlap between CP , PVL, prematurity, and strabismus is known.41 
Occipital lobe pathology may result in cortical visual impairment. 
Strabismus is important to recognize early to avoid amblyopia. 
Hearing deficits may be conductive and/or sensorineural in etiol-
ogy. Children with CP caused by kernicterus or TORCH infec -
tions should be screened for sensorineural hearing loss.
Elimination
In CP , there is a high incidence of bladder and bowel dysfunction 
(BBD), with continence achieved later than peers. BBD includes 
daytime and nighttime enuresis, urinary urgency or frequency, 
encopresis (fecal soiling), chronic and functional constipation, and 
pain in the lower abdominal area and pelvis. 5 BBD may result 
from impaired processing of sensory feedback from bladder and 
rectum, incoordination of pelvic floor muscles during elimination,

tivity. In CP , other factors affecting BBD and age at toilet training 
are cognitive ability, motor function level, and impairment of  
the autonomic system. 5 Urinary retention can increase chances

is generated, vesicoureteral reflux with resultant hydronephrosis 
and pelviectasis may occur. Clean intermittent catheterization is  
required when children are experiencing chronic urinary reten -
tion with urinary stasis. Anticholinergic treatments for dystonia 
or sialorrhea may trigger urinary retention. Urinary retention  
and urolithiasis can also produce discomfort and exacerbate  
hypertonicity.
Musculoskeletal Pain and Osteopenia
Pain in CP has been classified as nociceptive, nociplastic, neuro -
pathic, or mixed neuropathic/nociplastic.111 T﻿here is growing evi-
dence for both inflammatory and centralized pain mechanisms. 32 
Recurrent musculoskeletal pain in children with CP is common, 
with a linear correlation with higher GMFCS level and age. Chil-
dren who are less mobile are at increased risk for contracture and 
bony deformity with associated musculoskeletal pain. Common 
sites of musculoskeletal pain include the hips, spine, knee, and 
foot-ankle complex. A recent systematic review by Ostojic et al. 
on pharmacologic and surgical pain management in children and 
adolescents with CP found no scientific evidence to support the 
efficacy of interventions, such as different surgical procedures, 
botulinum neurotoxin type A (BoNT-A) injections, intrathecal 
baclofen (ITB) therapy, oral and inhaled drug therapies, and  
analgesic injections. Further research is recommended. T﻿he same 
applies to nonpharmacologic methods, such as physiotherapy, 
massage, environmental adjustments, and occupational therapy.88
As people with CP age, they may develop secondary condi -
tions, such as joint dislocations, contractures, osteoarthritis, ste -
nosis, spondylolysis/listhesis, and neuropathy. T﻿hese secondary 
conditions may result in chronic pain syndromes, which may  
be perpetuated by depression and other psychosocial factors. 126  
A recent systematic review and meta-analysis looking at pain in 
adults with CP estimated the incidence of chronic pain in adults 
with CP to be 70% compared to 20% in general population. 
Chronic pain is experienced across all GMFCS levels and is  
associated with several adverse quality of life and functional out -
comes. Lower back, hips, legs, feet, and neck were identified as 
the most painful locations in adults. 111 Chronic pain in adults 
with CP often goes underrecognized and undertreated. Patients 
with pain and CP are more likely to be exposed to opioids for 
longer periods of time than the non-CP population raising sig -
nificant concerns about opioid use in CP .111
Children with CP are at risk for osteopenia and related fragility 
fractures. Risk factors for osteoporosis include decreased weight 
bearing, use of anticonvulsants, malnutrition, and decreased sun -
light exposure. Supplementation with calcium and vitamin D is 
routinely used to improve bone density, but the effects on preven-
tion of fragility fractures are unclear. In those who are nonambula-
tory, it is recommended that their serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D level 
be 30 to 40 ng/mL.119 Bisphosphonates are effective in improving 
bone mineral density but are not without risk, and this intervention 
is usually reserved for children who have experienced a fragility frac-
ture. Because the long-term side effects with use of bisphosphonates 
are unknown, additional research is required before they become 
routinely used.29 Standing frames are used to facilitate weight bear-
ing in children with CP who are nonambulators. A few studies  
report a positive effect on bone density of standing for an average of 
30 to 60 minutes a day without it being statistically significant.119
Therapeutic Management
T﻿he treatment of a person with CP requires a multidisciplinary 
team that understands the different presentations of this condi -
tion, the motor control abnormalities, and the primary and sec -
ondary conditions associated with its multisystem involvement. 
T﻿he team composition may vary according to the child’s needs.  
It may include physiatrists, orthopedic surgeons, neurologists, 
neurosurgeons, developmental medicine specialists, physical thera-
pists, occupational therapists, speech-language pathologists, ortho-
tists, nutritionists, and social workers, among others. Additionally, 
it is important the family and child be active team members when 
setting priorities and goals within the context of the level of  
impairments and the child’s age.
Because of the WHO ICF-CY recommendation that disease 
treatments be focused mainly on the individual’s level of activity 
and participation, there has been a rising paradigm shift in the 
treatment of CP . T﻿he previous focus on alleviating the associated 
motor impairments has transitioned into discussions of how dif -
ferent treatment options will affect function. 17 T﻿he goal of any 
therapeutic intervention is to encourage the child’s maximal  
potential in motor, cognitive, and social realms.85
Childhood Disabilities and Education
T﻿here are numerous special education laws ensuring that indi -
viduals of all ages receive educational services in the United States. 
Table 48.5 summarizes pertinent legislation.47
Overall, physiatrists should be familiar with three terms that 
have evolved. Early intervention programs  are government-spon -
sored programs that are tasked to evaluate children at risk or with 
delays in one or more areas of function between the ages of 0 and 
36 months. 67 Services include therapies, nutrition monitoring, 
care coordination, audiovisual, and social work, among others. 
After 36 months of age, children with disabilities may qualify to 
receive ongoing services through their local public school system. 
A 504 plan ensures that appropriate classroom accommodations 
will be made for children who do not qualify for special educa -
tion classes. An individualized education program  ensures that 
classroom accommodations are provided, along with a document 
provided to the family with present level of functioning, short- 
and long-term goals, specific educational services needed, par -
ticipation in regular educational programs, and assurance that 
the necessary services are available. 47
Therapy Interventions
T﻿here are many forms of therapy interventions. T﻿herapists use a 
combination of approaches while tailoring their therapy program 
to each child’s needs. Novak et al., in a 2019 systematic review, 
highlight what works and what does not to inform decision mak-
ing and highlight areas for more research ( Fig. 48.6).80 T﻿he main 
goal of all therapies is to promote and facilitate development in all 
domains of function. 3 T﻿he setting and frequency of formal ther-
apy sessions vary. T﻿he most common therapy settings are school-
based, outpatient models with a frequency of one to three times a 
week per discipline. Other models include an intensive “burst” of 
therapy through camps or day rehabilitation programs. Regardless 
of the setting, it is imperative that the family and child are

Stretching
Stretching is a component of therapy programs; its goal is to  
reduce the risk of contracture development as a result of muscle 
imbalances and hypertonicity. Sustained stretching can be achieved 
through the use of positioning devices, orthoses, and serial casting. 
A systematic review examining the effects of casting, either alone 
or in combination with BoNT-A, on equinus in children with CP 
revealed no strong and consistent evidence that combining casting 
and BoNT-A is superior to using either intervention alone.
8 Data 
indicate that children tolerate casting better when it is applied  
4 weeks after toxin injection. 80
Strengthening
Historically, exercise programs that included strengthening were 
contraindicated in CP . A systematic review of the effectiveness of 
strength-training programs in CP reported increased strength 
without any negative effects of increased spasticity or reduced 
ROM. Indirectly, some studies have reported increased participa-
tion and improved self-esteem.
21 Moreau et al., in a meta-analysis 
looking at rehabilitation interventions to improve gait speed, 
found strength training in CP , regardless of the type and devices 
used, does not result in clinically meaningful changes in gait 
speed. Preliminary work on resistance training for muscle power, 
such as velocity training, was shown to be an effective means of 
increasing gait speed compared with traditional strength train-
ing.
71 High-intensity interval training in some studies has shown 
improved physical fitness, cardiometabolic risk biomarkers, men-
tal health, and cognitive performance in children with a spectrum 
of developmental disabilities, including CP .
95
Aerobic Exercise
Historically, exercise programs that included aerobic exercise were 
contraindicated in CP . A Cochrane review looking at exercise  
intervention in CP concluded that aerobic exercise may result in a 
small improvement in gross motor function in the short and inter-
mediate term and may improve physical activity without adverse 
events. Improvements in aerobic fitness have been described with 
45 minutes four times a week of “high intensity” activities such as 
wheelchair sports, swimming, matt exercises, or cycling. None of 
the studies have reported outcomes representing the ICF model of 
increased activity and participation.
100,105

Constraint-induced movement therapy (CIMT) is a treatment for 
hemiparesis to improve motor function in the affected upper 
limb. In infants and children with hemiplegic or unilateral CP , the 
unaffected limb is restrained with a removable cast, typically for  
3 weeks, and the child undergoes intensive, structured therapy in

Hand Arm Bimanual Intensive Therapy and Hand Arm 
Bimanual Intensive Therapy–Including Lower Extremity
HABIT and HABIT-ILE encompass bimanual upper extremity 
training with activities involving the trunk and lower extremities. 
It was initially designed for unilateral CP . However, recent studies 
have shown improvement in activities of daily living and dexterity 
in children with bilateral CP . T﻿hese improvements were retained 
6 months after initial treatment.30
Functional Electrical Stimulation
Neuromuscular electrical stimulation (NMS) is the application 
of an electrical current of sufficient intensity to elicit muscle 
contraction. When applied during a functional activity, it is  
referred to as functional electrical stimulation (FES) . An example 
is FES-assisted cycling. T﻿here is evidence to support NMS and 
FES.53,80
Robotic and Partial Body Weight Support  
Treadmill Training
T﻿he robotic and partial body weight support treadmill training 
(PBWSTT) forms of therapy involve a repetitive and task-specific 
approach to facilitate attainment of stepping and locomotion to 
achieve a more normalized gait pattern based on current theories 
of motor learning. T﻿he setting includes an overhead harness sys -
tem used to support the child’s body weight on a treadmill, while 
the therapist or a robot facilitates the kinematic, kinetic, and 
temporal features of walking. Although evidence of benefits from 
PBWSTT and robotic therapy has emerged in adults with stroke 
and spinal cord injury, several recent systematic reviews showed 
limited evidence to support the use of PBWSTT in children with 
CP . Despite reported improvements in gross motor function, 
functional status, walking performance, and gait parameters, sta -
tistical significance was not reached in most of the studies.
74,96
Neuromodulation
T ranscutaneous spinal stimulation and transcranial motor stimu-
lation are neuromodulation therapies proven to be safe, noninva -
sive, and well tolerated. T﻿hey work by triggering neural network 
reorganization. T﻿here is emerging evidence to support their use in 
children and adolescents with CP .
Durable Medical Equipment
T﻿he goal in prescribing durable medical equipment (DME) for 
individuals with CP should focus on maximizing function,  
improving safety, and enabling independence using the ICF-CY 
model of health. Motor impairment is one of the top reasons  
for a mobility device in CP because exploration of the environ -
ment is imperative for cognitive development that leads to maxi -
mizing functional independence. Supportive or adaptive seating 
systems and standing frames can facilitate a developmentally  
appropriate upright posture, strengthening, flexibility across the 
lower extremities, weight bearing/bone density, upper limb func-
tion, communication, and feeding by freeing the child’s hands  
to perform bimanual tasks, improving breath support, and opti -
mizing the head and trunk position to facilitate a safe swallow. 
Specialized seating devices are available for sitting on the floor  
or toilet, feeding, and bathing, as well as for incorporation in a 
mobility device.
85
Use of a wheelchair becomes applicable when a child either 
outgrows commercially available strollers or additional support is 
necessary. T﻿he goal of supportive seating is to provide an upright 
seated posture to facilitate interaction with the environment and 
minimize deforming forces secondary to postural abnormalities 
(Fig. 48.7
). T﻿h e clinician must consider both the seating goals and 
the family’s needs. One decision unique to pediatrics is whether 
to use either a conventional wheelchair base or a stroller base for 
the mobility device. Power mobility can be pursued in children as 
young as 18 months old if they have fair motor control, no visual 
deficits, and good cognition.
85
For children with adequate head and trunk support, a gait 
trainer, walker, or crutches might help facilitate gait training and 
provide external support and improve upright posture. T﻿he gait 
trainer is a wheeled walker with a sling seat and various support 
options, which allows the child to propel the device without  
necessarily having a coordinated, reciprocal gait pattern. For the 
dependent child and adult, families might benefit from equip -
ment to facilitate transfers and floor recovery, such as a mobile 
mechanical lift or overhead lift device.
85
T﻿he production of speech, language, and gesture for commu -
nication is also commonly affected in CP because of motor,  
sensory, and intellectual impairments. Various applications of 
technology have been used to maximize function. In augmenta -
tive communication, meaningful communication and expression 
of needs are facilitated with the use of computers, tablets, switch 
devices, signboards, and similar adaptive equipment.
85
Splinting and orthoses are commonly used in CP to manage 
spastic but flexible dynamic deformities of the extremities.

T﻿here are a variety of passive and dynamic splints. Orthoses must 
be tailored to the child’s age, deformity, motor control, and toler-
ance. With lower limb orthoses, the clinician should clearly iden-
tify the gait deviation and goals to be addressed, with special 
consideration of ankle-foot alignment, ROM, and tone. Ankle-
foot orthoses have been demonstrated to decrease the energy cost 
of walking in children with CP , compared with barefoot walking, 
and to improve gait parameters of stride length and velocity.
85
Management of Hypertonia
Hypertonia is one of the most prevalent comorbidities of CP 
that affect function. T﻿he most common forms observed in CP 
include spasticity, dystonia, and a combination of both. 50,103 
Spasticity is defined as velocity-dependent resistance, whereas 
dystonia is a more dynamic movement disorder, which involves 
an involuntary alteration in pattern of muscle activation during 
voluntary movement. It may fluctuate in presence and severity 
over time.
109 Learning to differentiate between them can guide 
goal setting and intervention. Hypertonia can be focal or gener-
alized and can affect the limbs and trunk. If left untreated, it  
can interfere with function and development. Hypertonia can 
lead to secondary musculoskeletal complications such as joint  
contractures and dislocations. It can also be a significant source 
of pain.
22
Management of hypertonia in children with CP is challenging, 
and no standard approach exists. T﻿he importance of physical 
therapy, orthoses, and casting has been stressed by a consensus 
group, but these interventions can be augmented with oral medi-
cations as well as chemodenervation with BoTN, phenol, or both. 
Neurosurgical procedures, including ITB and selective dorsal rhi-
zotomy (SDR), can also be considered in carefully selected popula-
tions. Less commonly, intraventricular baclofen (IVB), deep brain 
stimulation (DBS), and selective ventral rhizotomy can be consid-
ered in more severe or specific cases. Often, different combinations 
of these treatments are used to optimize treatment of hypertonia. 
T reatment must be tailored to goals and may change with growth 
and development. Goals should be identified before initiation of 
any treatment. Any spasticity-reducing treatment option should 
translate into improved mobility, self-care, skin breakdown preven-
tion, ease of care, or comfort and should be determined in conver-
sation with the patient and/or family.
Oral Medications
Generalized hypertonia is often treated with oral medications. 
Some of the most common medications include baclofen, diaze -
pam, dantrolene sodium, tizanidine, clonazepam, and clonidine. 
All of these, except dantrolene, can cause sedation because they 
exert their effects through the central nervous system.
18,109 An 
evidence-based practice parameter looking at the pharmacologic 
treatment of generalized spasticity in children and adolescents 
with CP found limited studies and insufficient data to support  
or refute the use of any of these drugs on a long-term basis.
18 
Pediatric dosing is quite variable, and the side effects and risks of 
these medications limit their use.
T﻿he treatment of generalized secondary dystonia in children 
with CP is more challenging because it responds poorly to oral 
medications. Commonly used drugs include trihexyphenidyl  
hydrochloride (Artane), oral baclofen, benzodiazepines, gabapen-
tin, clonidine, and levodopa-carbidopa (Sinemet). A recently 
published clinical practice guideline about pharmacologic and 
neurosurgical management of dystonia in CP by Fehlings et al. 
makes tiered recommendations about these agents. T﻿hey suggest 
baclofen as first line for generalized dystonia and clonidine and/
or gabapentin as second choices for severe cases.
28 T﻿he scientific 
evidence for the clinical use of trihexyphenidyl in children with 
CP remains equivocal.
106 Adverse events such as sedation, dizzi -
ness, and dry mouth are common and can limit its tolerance. ITB 
has been found to be more effective but at higher doses than what 
is required for treatment of spasticity

Chemodenervation
Intramuscular BoNT injections and chemical alcohol (CA),  
including phenol and ethanol neurolysis, are used in the treat -
ment of focal hypertonicity. 37 Both forms of chemodenervation 
(BoNT and CA) can be used at the same time to maximize the 
dose and number of muscles and in combination with systemic 
medications.
55 Typically, electrical stimulation, electromyogram 
(EMG), ultrasound guidance, or a combination of these is used 
for localization. A recent consensus statement was released dis -
cussing a tiered-based approach to consideration of BoNT use for 
patients with cerebral palsy.
128
BoNT is widely used in the management of CP . BoNT works by 
presynaptically blocking release of acetylcholine into the neuromus-
cular junction. Several BoNT-A products are on the market, but the 
only BoNT-B product is currently Myobloc. T﻿here are currently 
seven different serotypes of BoNT and at least five commercial 
preparations (e.g., Botox, Dysport, Xeomin, Myobloc, Jeuveau, 
Daxxify). Of the commercial preparations, more evidence is avail-
able for BoNT-A. Dosage and dilution guidelines have not been 
established for any of the commercial preparations, but consensus 
statements and systematic reviews provide recommendations.
37,128 
T﻿he child’s age, weight, severity of hypertonia, and muscle localiza-
tion are taken into consideration when determining dosage. T﻿he 
period of clinically useful relaxation is usually 12 to 16 weeks, and it 
is recommended that injections be spaced a minimum of 3 months 
apart because of concern for neutralizing antibody formation.
37 
Adverse events related to BoNT are rare and include injection site 
pain, fatigue, and excessive weakness of the injected muscle. Some 
cases of dysphagia and urinary incontinence have been reported.
18
BoNT treatment is appropriate for hypertonia in the form of 
spasticity and/or dystonia in all functional GMFCS levels I to V 
if the goal setting is adapted to the specific problems.
128 T﻿he main 
indication is hypertonia that hinders function and that if left 
untreated will become a fixed (rather than dynamic) contracture. 
Ideally, BoNT treatment should start at a young age when gait 
patterns and motor function are still flexible, allowing for gross 
motor function learning, including strengthening of antagonist 
muscle groups during the time window of tone reduction.
37 How-
ever, more recent discussion raises concern for the potential of 
progressive atrophy related to frequent recurrent injections.
73
Before BoNT was developed and commercialized, phenol  
motor point blocks were the only available form of focal che -
modenervation. CAs work by proteolytically causing neurolysis. 
Motor point blocks, in which medicine is injected into motor 
points within the muscle belly, can be performed with electrical 
stimulator alone, while true motor nerve blocks can be performed 
with ultrasound guidance. CAs lyse tissue, causing injections to 
be far more painful, and often require sedation. Nerves that have 
limited sensory fibers, such as the musculocutaneous, obturator, 
or pectoral nerves, can be safely targeted. Injecting other nerves 
has a higher risk of developing dysesthesias because of sensory  
fiber injury. In contrast to Botox, CAs are inexpensive, and results 
can last between 6 and 18 months.
55
Intrathecal Baclofen Therapy
ITB is most often used to treat children with generalized spasticity 
or generalized moderate-to-severe dystonia.
2 It is a US Food and 
Drug Administration–approved method to treat spasticity of  
cerebral or spinal origin. Currently, indications for the use of  
ITB include tone that is thought to interfere with function or the 
ability to provide care, modified Ashworth scores above 3, and de-
finable goals for spasticity reduction. ITB is delivered through a 
programmable pump placed in the subcutaneous tissue or myofas-
cial layer of the abdomen connected to the intrathecal space at the 
desired spinal level via a tunneled catheter. T﻿his method allows the 
delivery of smaller doses of baclofen (micrograms) intrathecally, 
thus reducing the side effect profile seen with the oral form of ba-
clofen (milligrams). T﻿he dose can be titrated to a desired therapeu-
tic response. A screening lumbar bolus dose of baclofen can be used 
to evaluate medication responsiveness. T﻿he pump requires replace-
ment every 6 to 7 years for battery replacement. For patients with 
more severe dystonia, intraventricular catheter placement (IVB) can 
be considered, though there may be increased risk for catheter mi-
gration and worsening obstructive sleep apnea compared to ITB.
A number of reports have noted that ITB reduces hypertonic -
ity, and the effect continues long term. Reports have attempted

reported improvements in positioning, activities of daily living, 
transfers, sleep, oral motor skills, hand use, and comfort. Motor

pediatric evaluation of disabilities inventory, and functional inde-

Others have found no improvement.57,60
Potential complications of ITB include infections, cerebrospi -
nal fluid leaks, and catheter problems such as disconnection, 
migration, fracture, or kinking. Abrupt withdrawal is a medical 
emergency and may present as increased tone, spasms, diaphore -
sis, agitation, and pruritus. If untreated, it can progress to rhab -
domyolysis and multisystem failure. T reatment includes high 
doses of oral baclofen as well as benzodiazepines and cyprohep -
tadine. T﻿he latter alleviates the pruritus related to ITB with -
drawal. Intrathecal baclofen through a lumbar drain can also be 
used in treatment of severe withdrawal. Acute complications  
include urinary retention, whereas constipation and weight gain 
tend to be more chronic complications.2,57 For these reasons, it is 
very important to verify that caregivers and family have the  
resources needed to comply with the longitudinal care of the ITB 
therapy and its potential complications.
Deep Brain Stimulation
DBS involves a stereotactic implantation of electrodes into the basal 
ganglia and a programmable pulse generator implanted subcutane-
ously in the infraclavicular region. DBS is the treatment of choice in 
primary dystonias. In CP , the majority of persons have generalized 
secondary dystonia (typically quadriplegics with GMFCS levels 
IV–V). ITB is the treatment of choice in this population; however, 
in those who do not respond adequately to ITB, DBS can be con-
sidered. T﻿he response is less than that seen in primary dystonia; some 
studies have reported only 30% to 33% reduction in different dys-
tonia rating scales, such as the Burke-Fahn-Marsden scale.58,110
Selective Dorsal Rhizotomy
SDR is a surgical procedure used for spasticity. T﻿he surgical tech-
nique involves single or multilevel laminectomies exposing the 
nerve roots and selectively cutting a percentage of the dorsal 
rootlets with abnormal response with the aid of electrophysiologic 
monitoring. For functional outcomes, the ideal candidate for 
SDR is a child between the ages of 3 and 8 years old with diplegic 
CP and predominantly spastic tone (typically GMFCS levels I–
III), sufficient underlying strength, good selective motor control, 
and minimal contractures. Positive preoperative functional pre -
dictors for a good SDR outcome include the ability to rise from a 
squatted position with minimal support and a younger child’s 
ability to crawl on hands and knees or tall kneel. 117 T﻿he presence 
of ataxia or moderate to severe dystonia is a relative contraindica-
tion because both conditions could be exacerbated and unmasked 
after SDR. A degree of lower limb weakness can be unmasked 
postoperatively by reducing the spasticity, making intensive phys-
ical therapy a necessity. Children must have the cognitive and 
social capacity for such an intensive intervention. 77
Positive outcomes of SDR include reduced spasticity, increased 
ROM, improved gait pattern and kinematics, increased speed, 
and decreased oxygen cost, making gait more efficient. 93,125 A 
meta-analysis of three randomized controlled trials evaluating the 
outcome of SDR in children with spastic diplegia showed that 
SDR plus physical therapy is more efficacious than physical 
therapy alone in reducing spasticity and has a small positive effect 
on gross motor function.65 A study looking at 136 patients retro-
spectively showed that spasticity decreased substantially at the 
major muscle groups: hip flexors, adductors, hamstrings, and 
rectus femoris. T﻿here was less reduction in the plantar flexor 
group, which may be functionally beneficial for joint stabilization 
and power production. Long-term outcomes at 5 and 20 years 
after SDR in children show an improvement in the GMFM and 
gait pattern.125 If done at an early age, SDR can reduce bony de -
formity, decreasing the need for orthopedic surgery in about 35% 
of those young children. Long-term complications such as sensory 
dysfunction, bladder or bowel dysfunction, or back pain are infre-
quent.68 SDR can be an alternative in nonambulatory GMFCS 
level IV to V children with severe spasticity if goals are to improve 
care and comfort. Combined dorsal and ventral rhizotomies have 
been performed in a few children with severe spasticity and dys -
tonia who were not candidates for ITB. 2
Orthopedic Management
Children with CP have abnormalities in muscle tone, strength, 
balance, and selective motor control as part of positive and nega -
tive features of the upper motor neuron syndrome. We now know 
that the muscles of children with CP are shorter and smaller and 
contain fibers of reduced diameter. T﻿his results in a muscle with 
smaller force-generating area and partially explains muscle weak -
ness in CP . Additional intrinsic muscle differences in CP are  
described in Fig. 48.8.38 T﻿hese abnormalities in muscle tone and 
weakness restrict the ROM of affected joints and, when coupled 
with musculoskeletal growth, result in muscle contractures and 
associated bony deformities.7,38
T﻿he introduction of the GMFCS, Functional Mobility Scale 
(FMS), and ICF has impacted orthopedic surgery. Surgeries de -
signed to improve ambulation are preferred in patients at GM -
FCS levels I to III, whereas surgeries aimed toward improving 
care and comfort are performed in those with GMFCS levels IV 
to V . Orthopedic surgical procedures should be considered in 
the presence of pain, contractures that hinder function or inter -
fere with hygiene, subluxation/dislocation of joints (most com -
monly the hips), and rotational problems or lever arm dysfunc -
tion, causing gait problems. T﻿here are four major types of 
orthopedic surgeries in CP: muscle releases and lengthening, 
tendon transfers, osteotomies, and arthrodesis. 17 T﻿he timing of 
surgical intervention is determined by central nervous system 
maturation, ambulation potential, and the rate at which the 
deformity is developing. Graham et al. have suggested delaying 
any orthopedic surgical intervention until ages 7 to 9 years be -
cause of an elevated risk of recurrence unless there is evidence of 
hip subluxation. Before this age, the focus should be therapies 
along with tone management. 37,38 Remember that orthopedic 
surgery in and of itself has no effect on motor control, strength, 
or muscle tone. T﻿he brief reduction in hypertonia is the direct 
result of the temporary decrease in muscle tension that involves 
the Golgi tendon apparatus and the muscle spindle. It is there -
fore of great importance to use a comprehensive team that ad -
dresses tone management presurgery and postsurgery. 17
Hips
It is estimated one in three children with CP will have hip dis -
placement. T﻿he highest risk is in the nonambulatory population, 
as high as 90% in GMFCS level V , but it does not correlate with 
motor type.38,46 T﻿he pathophysiology relates to the muscle imbal-
ance between the strong hip adductors/flexors and the weak hip 
abductors/extensors. Another factor involved in the pathophysiol-
ogy is the lack of weight bearing through the hip joint in nonam-

progression of migration percentage.

acetabular index over 30 degrees are likely to need further treat -

ambulatory child with CP , the goal of preserved ambulation re -
quires a contained and stable hip. In the nonambulatory child with 
CP , the goals include prevention of dislocation, maintenance of 
sitting balance, facilitation of hygiene, prevention of pain, preven-
tion of skin breakdown, and prevention of progressive scoliosis.

Lower Limb
In CP , positive and negative features of the upper motor neuron 
syndrome, delays in gross motor skill acquisition, and atypical 
gait patterns lead to further deformity. A diplegic sagittal gait pat-
tern classification has been developed ( Fig. 48.10), which helps 
define gait pathology, potential causes, and treatment strategies.
89 
Scissoring gait or excessive hip adduction and stiff knee gait or 
overactive rectus femoris are additional gait abnormalities that 
may coexist in this classification. T﻿hree-dimensional gait-and-
motion analysis aids in surgical planning and provides valuable 
kinematic, kinetic, and EMG information to further describe 
these gait abnormalities.
Hypertonia results in muscle contractures. In CP , biarticular 
muscles are more commonly contracted (e.g., psoas, rectus femo-
ris, hamstrings, gastrocnemii) than the monoarticular muscles.
38,78 
Before any orthopedic surgical intervention, tone management is 
recommended especially at an early age. In patients who are good 
candidates for ITB therapy or rhizotomy, the preference has been 
for those treatments to be pursued first because the need for  
orthopedic surgery could be minimized.
78

Orthopedic surgeries for contractures include lengthening and 
transfers. Typically, these procedures are more effective if integrated 
into a single-event multilevel surgery (SEMLS). SEMLS is defined as 
corrections of soft tissue and/or bony deformities at a minimum of 
two anatomic levels during a single operative event. T﻿he advantage of 
a SEMLS is one hospital admission and recovery period for multiple 
interventions.
61 T﻿h e goal is to improve the biomechanical impair -
ments that limit walking activity and community participation.
Church et al. reported that on average children recover base-
line walking activity after low-burden surgery relatively quickly  
(3 months). T﻿he recovery of baseline walking activity after high-
burden surgery requires an average of 13 months.15 Patient factors 
such as age, GMFCS level, and rehabilitation protocols have an 
impact on this timeline.
Abnormal long-bone torsions (lever arm dysfunction), such  
as persistent femoral anteversion, are also a result of abnormal 
muscle forces and delays in walking. Another less common  
example that is acquired later in development is tibial torsion.  
If left untreated, lever arm dysfunction will progress to further 
malalignment of the bone and unwanted gait compensations 
(Table 48.7).
33 In the nonambulatory cohort, goals of tenotomies 
include facilitating sitting (hamstrings) and perineal care (adduc-
tors), maintaining braceable feet (Achilles tendon, gastrocnemius-
soleus) to promote weight bearing, and preventing further hip 
migration (psoas and adductors) with or without bony surgery.
In CP , the development of the foot typically is not complete 
until the child is 7 to 8 years old.
In children with CP , spasticity at the ankle plantar flexors usu-
ally peaks at around age 5 years often with equinus contractures 
by age 5 to 8 years that worsen if untreated. Planovalgus foot 
deformities are common in young children less than age 5 years 
with most improving toward a normal foot posture by age 5 to  
7 years, then plateauing, and becoming more severe with adoles -
cent growth and weight gain. Varus foot deformities tend to per -
sist in hemiparetic CP . However, in those with diplegic CP , they 
usually overcorrect into planovalgus without intervention.

Upper Limb
T one management and orthopedic intervention for the upper limb 
in CP follow a similar treatment algorithm to that of the lower 
limb.
85 Conservative treatment with therapy, splinting (functional 
and passive orthoses), casting, oral medications, chemodenerva -
tion, and neurolysis are used to preserve ROM and delay muscle 
contractures. Upper limb surgical procedures are recommended to 
improve function, ease of care in patients with severe contractures, 
and sometimes cosmesis. Common functional impairments include 
deficiencies in sensation, pinch, grasp/release, and reach. T﻿he pat-
tern of joint contractures usually involves flexor synergy: shoulder 
internal rotation, elbow flexion, forearm pronation, wrist and  
finger flexion, thumb-in-palm deformity, and swan-neck deformi-
ties. T﻿he ideal candidate for surgical intervention would be a  
motivated child with volitional use of the hand, spasticity without 
fixed contractures, and reasonable sensory function, including 
stereognosis, proprioception, and light touch. Patients with sig -
nificant athetoid movements and dystonia often do not benefit 
from upper limb surgery to improve function given the variability 
of the outcomes.
129 In this cohort, bony fusions and arthrodesis are 
offered to stabilize joints. Regarding surgical timing, grip strength 
and manual dexterity improve with age and maturity. Although 
surgery before age 8 years may lead to better use of the corrected 
hand, transfers may work better after age 6 years, when children 
can participate in postoperative therapy. T endon transfers across  
an open growth plate must allow for increased tension with skel -
etal growth to avoid deformity.
13 Preoperative evaluation should  
include a detailed physical examination (ROM, strength, and tone), 
use of a functional classification system, and standardized tests that 
evaluate proprioception, stereognosis, and two-point discrimina-
tion. Motion analysis and computer modeling of the upper limb are 
increasingly being used to aid in surgical planning, much like three-
dimensional gait analysis for the lower limbs (Table 48.8).
85
Spine
T﻿he reported incidence of scoliosis in CP is estimated to be 21% 
to 76%. A recent population study by Willoughby et al. found that 
the scoliotic severity (curves .40 degrees Cobb angle) inversely 
correlated with functional and ambulatory status, GMFCS, and 
MACS level and with a higher prevalence in those with mixed tone 
and dystonia.
132 It also found that these curves continued to prog-
ress into early adulthood.
Scoliotic curves have been characterized and divided into two 
groups:
Group 1: Curves are single thoracic or double thoracic and lum -
bar curves with level pelvis. T﻿his type of cur ve is typically  
associated with ambulatory patients (GMFCS II–III). 49
Group 2: Curves are long thoracolumbar or C-shaped curves with 
associated pelvic obliquity, typically associated with nonambu-
latory patients.49
T﻿he natural history of curve progression has shown that onset 
occurs from 3 to 10 years of age in those with GMFCS level IV 
to V , with rapid progression during the adolescent growth spurt. 
Bracing traditionally has a limited role in decreasing curve pro -
gression.
49 A physical examination should be performed every  
6 to 12 months, and a radiograph is obtained if a curve is  
detected. Observation is warranted for flexible curves less than  
40 degrees that do not compromise sitting balance. In most cases 
of scoliosis in CP , spinal instrumentation and fusion are consid -
ered because of significant curve progression, loss of sitting bal -
ance, and comfortable function.
49 Ideally, this can be delayed in 
children with flexible deformities until they approach skeletal 
maturity. Surgery with complete spinal fusion in young children 
can impair growth of the spine and thorax, affecting respiratory 
development and function.
39 Growing rods (e.g., MAGEC rods) 
can be used to correct the deformity while allowing for growth. It 
is important to note that surgical complications in spine fusion 
are high; therefore goal setting, patient selection, and preoperative 
preparation are of utmost importance.
Transition to Adulthood and Aging  
With Cerebral Palsy
Preparing for transition begins early in the care of the individual 
with CP . A child must be encouraged to become as independent 
as possible and to separate if appropriate from caregivers in a  
developmentally appropriate fashion. T﻿hr oughout the course of 
care for the child, the medical team is responsible for providing 
anticipatory guidance and education. Guidelines for the care of

children and adolescents with CP exist and can help primary care 
physicians with ensuring comprehensive assessments of medical 
and psychosocial issues encountered in the CP population, direc-
tion on management of associated conditions, educational and 
community support needs, and suggested referrals to specialists 
for specific issues.
76,97 Conversations should be fostered with 
families to encourage patient autonomy and avoid infantilization 
when appropriate and may include challenging conversations 
about sexual and mental health.
T﻿herapy services for older individuals with CP are indicated for 
specific goals that will enhance functional independence or exercise 
capacity. For some individuals, independent living with employ -
ment is very realistic. Others may thrive in a supported environ -
ment with some daily assistance but not require constant supervi-
sion or care. Children with significant cognitive and physical delays 
may require long-term support but may enjoy opportunities for 
socialization with peers other than their primary caregivers. Chil-
dren with disabilities may need encouragement and self-advocacy 
training. Coping skills and self-reliance are usually acquired through 
experiences leading up to the point of transition and cannot be 
simply taught once an individual reaches adulthood.
T ransition of health care occurs in phases and requires prepara-
tion, readiness, and handoffs. Care delivery models often differ  
in the pediatric and adult realms, and moving to a new group of 
providers can be anxiety provoking for many. T eens benefit from 
having advocates or champions to help with the transition process. 
Mindful practitioners collaborate with interested parties, including 
families, schools, and community agencies, to provide comprehen-
sive guidance.
120 Patient counseling needs to include reminders 
about preventative health measures and screening. Health condi -
tions affecting the general population certainly will occur in adults 
with CP also. T﻿here are also specific conditions that occur more 
frequently or earlier in the adult CP population as the result of 
aging and chronic alterations in biomechanics, such as cervical 
myelopathy and early development of degenerative joint disease.
Challenges exist for the CP population as a whole associated 
with aging. Research shows that adults with CP have a higher risk 
of neurologic conditions such as stroke, myelopathy, and demen-
tia.116 Musculoskeletal conditions may worsen, and energy con-
sumption may increase for tasks previously accomplished easily. 
Adults with CP may experience chronic pain and associated  
fatigue and depression.
116,132 Many adults with CP will worsen by 
one GMFCS level as they age; many lose their ability to ambulate 
independently.
116 It is important to avoid a downward spiral 
when an exacerbation of a health issue or painful condition limits 
activity and functional decline ensues. With a shift in focus in 
measuring participation and quality of life indicators, the roles of 
physical fitness and strengthening in adults with CP are being 
investigated in greater detail.
124
Summary
In summary, caring for a person with CP is a lifelong commit-
ment requiring ongoing medical management, therapies and sup-
port systems. A team of specialists which include physiatrists 
works with the person and family to address specific needs. Care 
should be family centered and goal directed.
Ongoing monitoring of health, function and addressing  
potential complications accross teh life span is crucial.
